skill.name: q-and-a-prep-partner
skill.description: Predict challenging questions for presentations and prepare structured responses.
skill.summary: Generate predicted Q&A questions for academic presentations with structured response frameworks.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 50 static analysis findings are false positives. The 30 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code formatting in documentation files, not executable commands. The 4 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings flag dictionary keys and argparse calls as crypto operations. The 8 'system reconnaissance' findings flag Q&A question templates and audit metadata. The 4 'path traversal' and 2 'hardcoded URL' findings flag security checklist items and a source attribution link. The Python script is a benign Q&A question generator with no network calls, dangerous operations, or data exfiltration paths.

medium_findings.title: Markdown Code Formatting Flagged as Shell Backtick Execution
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 30 instances of markdown inline code and fenced code blocks as Ruby/shell backtick execution. All flagged locations in SKILL.md and references/audit-reference.md are markdown documentation formatting showing example Python commands (e.g., 'python -m py_compile scripts/main.py'). These are documentation, not executable code. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged lines 53, 56, 57, 112 in scripts/main.py and lines 3, 24 in SKILL.md as weak crypto. The flagged lines in main.py are RESPONSE_FRAMEWORKS dictionary keys (methodology, limitations, future) and argparse initialization. SKILL.md lines are YAML frontmatter and markdown text. No cryptographic operations exist anywhere in the skill. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 8 locations across 3 files as system reconnaissance. q-and-a-prep-partner_audit_result_v2.json lines 23, 125, 195, 225 are evaluation metadata fields. scripts/main.py lines 17, 18, 23 are Q&A question templates (e.g., 'Why did you choose {method} over alternatives?'). SKILL.md lines 58, 70, 151 are workflow descriptions and test case labels. No actual system probing. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md lines 128 and 131 as path traversal. These lines are security checklist items that document path traversal PREVENTION (e.g., '- [ ] No unauthorized file system access (../)' and '- [ ] Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'). The skill documents its own security controls. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Attribution False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md line 7 for a hardcoded URL. This is a source attribution link (https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills) pointing to the skill's own parent repository. It is a documentation reference, not a network call or data exfiltration endpoint. FALSE POSITIVE.

content.user_title: Prepare for Academic Presentation Q&A Sessions
content.value_statement: This skill helps you anticipate difficult questions before your academic presentation. It generates audience-appropriate questions with structured response frameworks so you can prepare clear answers.
actual_capabilities: Generates predicted questions across six categories: methodology, statistics, interpretation, limitations, future work, and comparison
Adapts question types based on audience type: general, peers, or experts
Provides structured response frameworks with a 4-step preparation guide for each question
Reads abstract text from a file or accepts direct topic input to generate context-aware questions
Supports configurable number of questions with configurable research field focus
limitations: Cannot predict questions for non-academic presentation formats such as sales pitches or product demos
Response frameworks are generic templates that require user adaptation for specific research contexts
Questions are generated using templates, not derived from deep understanding of the research content
Does not provide answers to the predicted questions, only frameworks for constructing responses

use_cases.title: Conference Presentation Preparation
use_cases.description: A researcher preparing for an academic conference uploads their abstract and selects the expert audience mode. The skill generates methodology and statistics questions to help them prepare for challenging peer review.

use_cases.title: Thesis Defense Practice
use_cases.description: A PhD candidate uses the skill to predict questions their committee might ask during a thesis defense. The skill generates limitation and future work questions to help them prepare critical responses.

use_cases.title: Lab Meeting Presentation
use_cases.description: A postdoc preparing for a lab meeting uses the peer audience mode to anticipate questions from colleagues. The skill generates interpretation and comparison questions to prepare for interdisciplinary feedback.
use_cases.target_user: Postdocs and researchers presenting at lab meetings

prompt_templates.title: Basic Q&A Preparation
prompt_templates.prompt: I am presenting on the topic of [TOPIC] in the field of [FIELD]. The audience will be [AUDIENCE_TYPE]. Please generate [N] predicted questions and provide response frameworks for each one.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user preparing for a standard presentation

prompt_templates.title: Abstract-Based Question Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Here is my abstract text: [ABSTRACT_TEXT]. I will be presenting this to a [AUDIENCE_TYPE] audience. Using the q-and-a-prep-partner skill, predict the most challenging questions I might receive and prepare response frameworks for each.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher with a completed abstract seeking targeted question prediction

prompt_templates.title: Expert Audience Deep Dive
prompt_templates.prompt: I am presenting my research on [TOPIC] to a panel of expert reviewers in [FIELD]. They will scrutinize my methodology and statistical approach. Use the q-and-a-prep-partner skill to generate the most rigorous methodological and statistical questions I should prepare for.
prompt_templates.scenario: Senior researcher preparing for expert peer review or grant panel

prompt_templates.title: Comprehensive Defense Preparation
prompt_templates.prompt: I am defending my research on [TOPIC] in the field of [FIELD]. The committee includes experts in methodology and statistics. Generate 15 questions across all categories including comparison with prior work and future research directions. For each question, provide a detailed response framework covering acknowledgment, key evidence, and anticipated follow-up questions.
prompt_templates.scenario: PhD candidate preparing for a comprehensive thesis defense

output_examples.input: I am presenting on CRISPR therapy for oncology to an expert audience.
output: Q: Can you elaborate on your proposed method approach?
Q: Was your sample size adequately powered?
Generated 10 potential questions for expert audience in oncology field.

output_examples.input: I am presenting my research on machine learning for climate modeling to peers.
output: Q: How do you interpret these findings in context of climate science?
Q: What are the next steps for this research?

best_practices: Provide the full abstract text for more context-aware questions that match your specific research content.
Select the audience type that matches your actual presentation setting to get the most relevant question categories.
Practice your responses out loud after reviewing each response framework to build confidence and fluency.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill for non-academic presentations such as business pitches or product demonstrations where question patterns differ significantly.
Do not rely solely on the generated response frameworks without adapting them to your specific research context and data.
Do not request an excessive number of questions (more than 20) as quality and relevance of predictions will decrease.

faq.question: What types of questions does this skill generate?
faq.answer: The skill generates questions across six categories: methodology, statistics, interpretation, limitations, future work, and comparison. The categories included depend on the selected audience type.

faq.question: How does the audience type affect question generation?
faq.answer: For expert audiences, the skill focuses on methodology, statistics, comparison, and limitations. For peers, it includes methodology, interpretation, future work, and limitations. For general audiences, it focuses on interpretation, future work, and limitations.

faq.question: Can I provide my abstract as input?
faq.answer: Yes. You can provide an abstract as a text file using the --abstract parameter or paste the abstract text directly using the --topic parameter. The skill will use the first 100 characters for context.

faq.question: Does this skill provide answers to the questions?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides predicted questions and response frameworks (structured outlines for constructing answers). You need to prepare the actual content of your responses based on your research.

faq.question: How many questions can I generate at once?
faq.answer: You can generate any number of questions by setting the --n-questions parameter. The default is 10 questions. For the best quality, we recommend requesting between 5 and 15 questions.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for non-academic presentations?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for academic presentations and research contexts. It may not be suitable for business pitches, product demonstrations, or other non-academic presentation formats.
